Setup.details: Displays the current version of Git installed on your system.
Configures the name that will be attached to your commits globally on the current machine.
Configures the email address that will be associated with your commits globally.
Specifies which text editor Git should use when it needs you to type a message (like for commits or rebases).
Shows all the configuration settings currently in effect for Git, including global and repository-specific ones.
Creates a new .git subdirectory in your current directory, turning it into a Git repository.

Working with Files.details: Displays the state of the working directory and the staging area, showing which changes have been staged, which haven't, and which files aren't being tracked.
Shows the differences between the files in your working directory and the last committed version (or the staged version).
Shows the differences between the files you have staged for the next commit and the last committed version.
Adds a specific file to the staging area, preparing it to be included in the next commit.

Working with Files.desc: Stage all modified and new files
Working with Files.details: Adds all new, modified, and deleted files in the current directory and subdirectories to the staging area.
Removes a file from both the working directory and the Git index (staging area).
Renames or moves a file, and automatically stages the change for the next commit.

Working with Files.desc: Show file modifications line by line
Working with Files.details: Displays the last commit that modified each line of a file, along with the author and timestamp.

Commits.desc: Commit staged changes with a message
Commits.details: Takes a snapshot of the staged changes and records them in the repository's history with a descriptive message.
Allows you to add new staged changes to the most recent commit or change its commit message.

Commits.desc: Amend the last commit without changing the message
Commits.details: Adds staged changes to the last commit while keeping the original commit message intact.
Lists all the commits made in the current branch, starting from the most recent.
Displays the commit history with each commit condensed into a single line showing its short hash and message.
Visualizes the commit history and branch structure using a text-based graph.

Commits.desc: Show details of a specific commit
Commits.details: Displays the detailed information and the code changes introduced by a specific commit hash.

Branches.details: Shows all the branches that exist in your local repository, highlighting the one you are currently on.
Creates a new pointer (branch) at the current commit, but does not switch to it.
Deletes a local branch only if it has already been fully merged into its upstream branch or the current branch.
Updates the files in your working directory to match the version stored in the specified branch and switches the active branch.

Branches.desc: Create and switch to a new branch
Branches.details: A shortcut command that creates a new branch and immediately switches your working directory to it.
The modern, more specific command for switching between branches, introduced to replace some uses of 'checkout'.

Tags.details: Displays all the tags (labels for specific commits) in the repository.
Creates a tag object that contains the tagger's name, email, date, and a message; useful for marking release points like v1.0.

Tags.desc: Push all tags to the remote repository
Tags.details: Transfers all your local tags to the remote server, as tags are not automatically pushed with 'git push'.

Remote Repositories.details: Creates a local copy of an existing remote repository, including all its history and branches.
Shows the names and URLs of the remote repositories that your local repository is connected to.

Remote Repositories.desc: Fetch updates from the remote repository
Remote Repositories.details: Downloads the latest changes from a remote repository but does not merge them into your local branches.
Updates your local list of remote branches, removing references to branches that have been deleted on the server.
A combination of 'git fetch' and 'git merge'; it downloads changes from the remote and integrates them into your current branch.

Remote Repositories.desc: Push local changes to the remote repository
Remote Repositories.details: Uploads your local commit history to the specified remote branch.

Remote Repositories.desc: Push and set the upstream branch
Remote Repositories.details: Pushes your branch to the remote and also sets up a tracking relationship so future 'git push/pull' commands know which remote to use.

Merging.desc: Merge a branch into the current one
Merging.details: Combines the history and changes from the specified branch into your currently active branch.

Merging.desc: Merge with a new commit object
Merging.details: Forces Git to create a merge commit even if a fast-forward merge is possible, preserving the history of the feature branch.
Combines all the commits from the feature branch into a single set of changes to be committed on the current branch.
Moves the entire base of your current branch to the tip of the specified branch, creating a linear history.

Merging.desc: Apply a specific commit to the current branch
Merging.details: Takes the changes introduced by a single specific commit from another branch and applies them to your current branch.

Undo & Reset.desc: Discard unstaged changes in a file
Undo & Reset.details: Reverts a file in your working directory to its last committed or staged state.
Removes a file from the staging area but keeps the changes in your working directory.
Creates a new commit that applies the exact opposite changes of a previous commit, effectively undoing it while preserving history.

Undo & Reset.desc: Undo the last commit but keep staged changes
Undo & Reset.details: Moves the branch pointer back by one commit, but leaves all the changes from that commit staged and ready to be recommitted.

Undo & Reset.desc: Undo the last commit but keep files as unstaged
Undo & Reset.details: Moves the branch pointer back one commit and unstages the changes, but keeps the actual file modifications in your working directory.

Undo & Reset.desc: Undo the last commit and discard all changes
Undo & Reset.details: Moves the branch pointer back and completely discards all changes in both the staging area and the working directory. Use with caution!

Undo & Reset.desc: Remove all untracked files and directories
Undo & Reset.details: Deletes files and folders in your working directory that are not being tracked by Git.

Stashing.details: Takes all your current uncommitted changes (both staged and unstaged) and saves them on a temporary stack for later use.
Shows all the sets of changes you have previously saved using 'git stash'.
Restores the changes from the most recent stash to your working directory but does not remove them from the stash list.

Stashing.desc: Apply and remove the most recent stash
Stashing.details: Restores the changes from the most recent stash and immediately deletes that stash from the list.
Permanently removes all stashed changes from your repository.
